content:
Stateside 1 — First appearance of First Person and the true reveal of Pathos, coincidence? I don’t think so. And what really sticks out to me as different is that it doesn’t display a traditional death or an expected one; it’s a death left over from the ‘real’ world, not the world everyone enters into upon deployment. You’ve beefed up the beginning of the collection with more pre-deployment pieces, and that gives the reader a different perspective then what the outside world might imagine the experience of all these adolescent people jetted into adulthood might be like. You are still playing like kids in these pieces; displaying even a play ‘death.’

Non-deployable Unit — When we remain in first person, I can’t help but think this is now the same character from “Stateside 1.”

Stateside 2 — The transition of the last line from “Non-deployable Unit” into the first line of this piece presents the shift of consciousness from one “I” to the other “I” - or, I can read these set of pieces from one singular perspective. And as a triptych, they work really well. My gut tells me Stateside 1 & 2 are pieces to lead with when sending work to agents because there is so much powerful emotion in them. Also, I want to mention the irony of the narrator from the previous story being deployed even though he promised his family that they wouldn’t be deployed book ended by two pieces that present two individuals who were they themselves non-deployable.
context: From document: Tomb of the Unknowns Notes — chunk 3 (chunk 3/4)
